section.title: The Samaritan Woman and the Official's Son
section.sectionRationale: From a learned Pharisee by night the Gospel turns to a Samaritan woman at noon, an outsider by race, gender, and reputation. Jesus crosses every barrier to offer her living water and to reveal Himself as the Messiah, and she becomes an evangelist to her town. The chapter closes with the second sign, the healing of an official's son at a distance, a man who simply takes Jesus at His word.
section.thesis: Jesus freely crosses the barriers of race, gender, and shame to give living water that becomes a spring of eternal life, revealing Himself as the Christ and the Savior of the world whom the Father seeks worshipers to worship in spirit and truth, and He is trusted at His bare word by an official whose son He heals from afar.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the request for a drink and the offer of living water; true worship in spirit and truth and Jesus' self-revelation as Messiah; the woman's witness and the Samaritans' faith in the Savior of the world; and the healing of the official's son, the second sign, received by faith in Jesus' word.

units.label: Living water at the well
units.note: a spring welling up to eternal life

verses.text: When Jesus realized that the Pharisees were aware He was gaining and baptizing more disciples than John
(although it was not Jesus who baptized, but His disciples),
He left Judea and returned to Galilee.
Now He had to pass through Samaria.
So He came to a town of Samaria called Sychar, near the plot of ground that Jacob had given to his son Joseph.
Since Jacob’s well was there, Jesus, weary from His journey, sat down by the well. It was about the sixth hour.
When a Samaritan woman came to draw water, Jesus said to her, “Give Me a drink.”
(His disciples had gone into the town to buy food.)
“You are a Jew,” said the woman. “How can You ask for a drink from me, a Samaritan woman?” (For Jews do not associate with Samaritans.)
Jesus answered, “If you knew the gift of God and who is asking you for a drink, you would have asked Him, and He would have given you {{hydorzon|living water}}.”
“Sir,” the woman replied, “You have nothing to draw with and the well is deep. Where then will You get this living water?
Are You greater than our father Jacob, who gave us this well and drank from it himself, as did his sons and his livestock?”
Jesus said to her, “Everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again.
But whoever drinks the water I give him will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give him will become in him a fount of water springing up to eternal life.”
The woman said to Him, “Sir, give me this water so that I will not get thirsty and have to keep coming here to draw water.”
Jesus told her, “Go, call your husband and come back.”
“I have no husband,” the woman replied. Jesus said to her, “You are correct to say that you have no husband.
In fact, you have had five husbands, and the man you now have is not your husband. You have spoken truthfully.”
“Sir,” the woman said, “I see that You are a prophet.
Our fathers worshiped on this mountain, but you Jews say that the place where one must worship is in Jerusalem.”
“Believe Me, woman,” Jesus replied, “a time is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem.
You worship what you do not know; we worship what we do know, for salvation is from the Jews.
But a time is coming and has now come when the true worshipers will worship the Father in {{pneuma|spirit}} and in truth, for the Father is seeking such as these to worship Him.
God is Spirit, and His worshipers must worship Him in spirit and in truth.”
The woman said, “I know that Messiah” (called Christ) “is coming. When He comes, He will explain everything to us.”
Jesus answered, “I who speak to you {{egoeimi|am He}}.”

units.label: The woman's witness and the Samaritans' faith

verses.text: Just then His disciples returned and were surprised that He was speaking with a woman. But no one asked Him, “What do You want from her?” or “Why are You talking with her?”
Then the woman left her water jar, went back into the town, and said to the people,
“Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Could this be the Christ?”
So they left the town and made their way toward Jesus.
Meanwhile the disciples urged Him, “Rabbi, eat something.”
But He told them, “I have food to eat that you know nothing about.”
So the disciples asked one another, “Could someone have brought Him food?”
Jesus explained, “My food is to do the will of Him who sent Me and to finish His work.
Do you not say, ‘There are still four months until the harvest’? I tell you, lift up your eyes and look at the fields, for they are ripe for harvest.
Already the reaper draws his wages and gathers a crop for eternal life, so that the sower and the reaper may rejoice together.
For in this case the saying ‘One sows and another reaps’ is true.
I sent you to reap what you have not worked for; others have done the hard work, and now you have taken up their labor.”
Many of the Samaritans from that town believed in Jesus because of the woman’s testimony, “He told me everything I ever did.”
So when the Samaritans came to Him, they asked Him to stay with them, and He stayed two days.
And many more believed because of His message.
They said to the woman, “We now believe not only because of your words; we have heard for ourselves, and we know that this man truly is the {{soter|Savior}} of the world.”

units.label: The official's son healed
units.note: the second sign, faith in the word

verses.text: After two days, Jesus left for Galilee.
Now He Himself had testified that a prophet has no honor in his own hometown.
Yet when He arrived, the Galileans welcomed Him. They had seen all the great things He had done in Jerusalem at the feast, for they had gone there as well.
So once again He came to Cana in Galilee, where He had turned the water into wine. And there was a royal official whose son lay sick at Capernaum.
When he heard that Jesus had come from Judea to Galilee, he went and begged Him to come down and heal his son, who was about to die.
Jesus said to him, “Unless you people see signs and wonders, you will never believe.”
“Sir,” the official said, “come down before my child dies.”
“Go,” said Jesus. “Your son will live.” The man took Jesus at His word and departed.
And while he was still on the way, his servants met him with the news that his boy was alive.
So he inquired as to the hour when his son had recovered, and they told him, “The fever left him yesterday at the seventh hour.”
Then the father realized that this was the very hour in which Jesus had told him, “Your son will live.” And he and all his household believed.
This was now the second sign that Jesus performed after coming from Judea into Galilee.

hydorzon.gloss: Living water. The phrase ordinarily meant fresh, flowing water rather than still cistern water, and the woman first hears it that way. Jesus means something greater: the gift of God, the life He gives by the Spirit, which becomes an inner spring welling up to eternal life. It answers a thirst that Jacob's well never could.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit. God is Spirit, and true worship is offered in spirit and in truth, not bound to a particular mountain or temple. The word points to worship that is inward and real, made possible by the Holy Spirit, the same Spirit Jesus links to the living water and the new birth. Place gives way to the Spirit and the truth who is Christ.

egoeimi.gloss: I am, or I am He. To the woman's expectation of Messiah, Jesus answers literally I am, the phrase that in John repeatedly echoes the divine name revealed to Moses, I AM. It is His plainest self-disclosure so far, given not to a scholar in Jerusalem but to a Samaritan woman at a well, and it anticipates the great I am sayings to come.

soter.gloss: Savior, deliverer, rescuer. The Samaritans confess Jesus as the Savior of the world, a title that in the Roman world was claimed by Caesar. John records outsiders grasping what many insiders miss: that Jesus saves not one nation only but the world, exactly the reach of God's love in giving His Son.

basic.context: Jesus takes the road through Samaria, land the Jews avoided, and rests at Jacob's well at noon. A Samaritan woman comes to draw water, and Jesus, crossing every social line, asks her for a drink and offers her living water. He gently exposes her broken past, and she raises the old dispute about where to worship. Jesus lifts worship above places to spirit and truth, then reveals plainly that He is the Messiah. She runs to tell her town, and many believe. The chapter ends with a royal official who simply trusts Jesus' word, Your son will live, and finds it so.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch how Jesus moves from a physical thing, water, to a spiritual reality, the life He gives, a pattern all through John. The key questions are, what is the living water, what is true worship, and what does faith look like. The answers: the living water is the Spirit and the life Jesus gives; true worship is in spirit and truth, centered on Christ; and faith takes Jesus at His word, as the official did, without needing to see first.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a few key terms. Jesus offers hydor zon, living water, that becomes a spring of eternal life. He teaches worship in pneuma, spirit, and truth, for God is Spirit. To the woman He says ego eimi, I am He, the divine self-disclosure. And the Samaritans confess Him soter, the Savior of the world.
basic.typology: Jacob's well, given by the patriarch, is surpassed by the water Jesus gives; the meeting at a well, where Isaac and Jacob found brides, hints at the bridegroom gathering His people. The harvest imagery gathers the ingathering of the nations that the prophets foresaw.
basic.christ: Christ reveals Himself as the giver of living water, the one greater than Jacob, the Messiah who tells all things, and the Savior of the world. He is trusted at His bare word by the official, and His word carries the power to give life at a distance.
basic.god: God is Spirit, and He is seeking worshipers, actively looking for people to draw near in spirit and truth. Salvation is from the Jews, yet God's reach is the whole world. The Father sends the Son, whose very food is to do His will and finish His work.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 55 invites the thirsty to come to the waters. Jeremiah 2 contrasts the fountain of living water with broken cisterns. John 7 unfolds the living water as the Spirit. 1 John 4 confesses the Son as the Savior of the world.
basic.oneStory: The gift of living water and the offer of true worship reach beyond Israel to a Samaritan outsider and her town, a first fruits of the nations. Jesus, greater than Jacob, is the Messiah and Savior of the world, and the harvest of all peoples that the prophets foresaw has begun to be gathered to Him.

context.literary: A long dialogue framed by two signs, structured by Jesus' move from the physical to the spiritual: water to living water, husbands to worship, food to the Father's will, fields to harvest. It contrasts pointedly with chapter 3: a man and a woman, a Jew and a Samaritan, night and noon, a teacher and an outcast. The healing of the official's son closes the movement as the second Cana sign, bookending the section with Cana.
context.historical: Jews and Samaritans shared a bitter, centuries-old estrangement over worship, the temple, and the Scriptures; Samaritans worshiped on Mount Gerizim. A respectable woman would draw water in the cool of morning or evening, not at noon, hinting at her isolation. Rabbis avoided public conversation with women, which explains the disciples' surprise. A royal official served in the household or administration of Herod Antipas.
context.canonical: The living water fulfills prophetic images of a fountain for the thirsty, Isaiah 55:1 and Jeremiah 2:13, and anticipates John 7:37-39, where the water is named as the Spirit. The turn from temple worship to worship in spirit and truth prepares for the new covenant reality of Christ as the true temple and the Spirit poured out. The Savior of the world confession situates John's Gospel in the wide scope of God's saving purpose for the nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus crossing every human barrier to give the living water of eternal life, to redefine true worship as worship of the Father in spirit and truth centered on Himself, and to commend a faith that takes Jesus at His word, as both the Samaritans and the official did.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes particular encounters, but it teaches abiding truths: Jesus gives the living water of the Spirit to all who ask, true worship is in spirit and truth rather than tied to a place, and saving faith rests on Jesus' word. The woman's witness models the call to bring others to Christ.

debates.question: What is the living water Jesus offers?

views.label: The Holy Spirit and the life He brings
views.case: John 7:37-39 explicitly identifies the living water with the Spirit given to believers; the water becomes an inner spring welling up to eternal life, the Spirit's work of new and unfailing life.

views.label: The salvation and eternal life Jesus gives
views.case: Others stress the gift more broadly as the salvation and eternal life Jesus grants, which the Spirit conveys; the emphasis falls on the satisfying, everlasting life found only in Christ.

debates.bearing: The two readings converge: the living water is the life of God given by the Spirit through Christ, a gift that permanently satisfies the deepest thirst.

debates.question: What does worship in spirit and in truth mean?

views.label: By the Holy Spirit and in accord with the truth in Christ
views.case: God is Spirit, and true worship is enabled by the Holy Spirit and shaped by the truth revealed in Jesus; it is no longer bound to Gerizim or Jerusalem but to Christ.

views.label: Inwardly sincere and truthful worship
views.case: Others read spirit and truth as heartfelt, genuine worship from the inner person, as opposed to mere external ritual tied to a location.

debates.bearing: Both are true and related: worship is now offered through the Spirit and centered on the truth who is Christ, from a sincere heart rather than a sacred place.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter unfolds through key terms. Jesus offers hydor zon, living water, which the woman hears as flowing spring water but which is the gift of God, the life of the Spirit welling up to eternal life. He teaches that God is pneuma, Spirit, so the true worshipers worship in spirit and in truth, freed from any single holy place. To the woman's hope of Messiah He answers ego eimi, I am He, the divine self-disclosure that runs through the Gospel. And the Samaritans, having heard for themselves, confess Him soter, the Savior of the world, a title of the widest reach.

typology.fulfillment: The greater water Jesus gives, which becomes an inner spring of eternal life, surpassing the well of Jacob, John 4:12-14.

typology.type: The betrothal meeting at a well
typology.fulfillment: Christ the bridegroom drawing His people, gathering even the Samaritan outsider, echoing the well scenes of Genesis 24 and 29.

typology.type: The ripe fields and the harvest
typology.fulfillment: The ingathering of the nations into the kingdom, beginning with the Samaritan town, John 4:35-38.

study.christ: Christ reveals Himself with unusual openness here. He is the giver of living water, greater than Jacob, whose gift satisfies forever and becomes a spring of eternal life. He is the one who knows the woman's whole life and yet welcomes her, exposing her sin without shaming her away. He answers her hope of Messiah with the words I am He, the divine self-naming. And He is confessed by outsiders as the Savior of the world, whose word alone heals the official's dying son at a distance, showing that His mere word gives life.
study.god: God is revealed as Spirit, unbounded by place, and as a seeking God: the Father is actively seeking worshipers to worship Him in spirit and truth. He is a God whose salvation comes through Israel yet reaches the whole world, gathering Samaritans and Gentiles into His purpose. His will is the very food of the Son, who came to finish the Father's work, and He is the God who gives life through the word of Jesus, honored by the faith that simply trusts what He has said.

crossReferences.note: My people have forsaken Me, the fountain of living water, and dug broken cisterns; Jesus offers the true living water.
Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters, the prophetic invitation Jesus fulfills.
Jesus identifies the rivers of living water with the Spirit whom believers would receive.
The Father has sent the Son to be the Savior of the world, the confession the Samaritans reach.

study.oneStory: The gift of living water and the offer of true worship break beyond the borders of Israel to a Samaritan woman and her town, a first fruits of the great ingathering of the nations. Jesus, greater than Jacob, reveals Himself as the Messiah and is confessed as the Savior of the world, and His word gives life even at a distance. The prophets' vision of a fountain for the thirsty and a harvest of all peoples begins to come true, as the Father seeks worshipers from every nation to draw near in spirit and truth through His Son.

quiz.q: What is the living water Jesus offers the Samaritan woman?

options.text: The gift of God, the life of the Spirit that becomes an inner spring welling up to eternal life.
options.note: Verses 10 and 14 describe a gift that satisfies forever and springs up to eternal life, later named as the Spirit in John 7.

options.text: Fresh flowing spring water rather than well water.
options.note: This is how the woman first hears it, but Jesus means the spiritual gift of eternal life, not literal water.

options.text: The eternal life and salvation Jesus gives.
options.note: True and close, but it does not name the Spirit and the inner spring that the passage and John 7 specify.

options.text: The water of Jacob's well, blessed for the patriarchs.
options.note: Jesus contrasts His water with Jacob's well, which leaves people thirsty again.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about true worship?

options.text: True worshipers worship the Father in spirit and in truth, for God is Spirit.
options.note: Verses 23 and 24 lift worship above any sacred location to spirit and truth.

options.text: Worship must happen only in Jerusalem, as the Jews said.
options.note: Jesus affirms salvation is from the Jews, but says a time has come when worship is bound to no place.

options.text: Worship is no longer tied to a mountain or temple but to the Father.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the heart of Jesus' teaching, worship in spirit and truth.

options.text: Any sincere worship of any god is acceptable.
options.note: Jesus ties true worship to the truth and to knowing the Father; not all worship is the same.

quiz.q: How does Jesus reveal His identity to the woman?

options.text: He plainly says I who speak to you am He, the Messiah.
options.note: Verse 26 records His direct self-disclosure as the Christ, using the words I am He.

options.text: He performs a miracle to prove who He is.
options.note: He reveals Himself by word, not by a miracle in this scene.

options.text: He tells her the history of her life, showing prophetic insight.
options.note: He does show supernatural knowledge, which leads her to call Him a prophet, but the climactic revelation is His own claim, I am He.

options.text: He lets her guess and never confirms it.
options.note: He does not leave it to guessing; He openly confirms He is the Messiah.

quiz.q: Why do the Samaritans finally believe in Jesus?

options.text: They hear Him for themselves and know He truly is the Savior of the world.
options.note: Verse 42 shows their faith moving from the woman's testimony to firsthand hearing of Jesus.

options.text: Only because the woman told them about Him.
options.note: Her testimony began it, verse 39, but they say they now believe because they have heard for themselves.

options.text: Because Jesus performed signs among them.
options.note: No sign is recorded in the town; they believe because of His message heard directly.

options.text: Because Jesus promised them political freedom.
options.note: There is no such promise; they confess Him as Savior of the world, not a political liberator.

quiz.q: What marks the faith of the royal official in the second sign?

options.text: He took Jesus at His word that his son would live and departed trusting it.
options.note: Verse 50 shows him believing Jesus' bare word before any evidence.

options.text: He believed only after seeing his son healed with his own eyes.
options.note: He believed the word first and set out; the confirmation came later on the road.

options.text: He trusted Jesus' promise and set out for home believing.
options.note: True, but it omits the striking point that he trusted the bare word before any proof.

options.text: He demanded that Jesus come and touch his son in person.
options.note: He first asked Jesus to come down, but then took Him at His word without His coming.

questions: Jesus crossed barriers of race, gender, and reputation to reach one woman. Who might you be overlooking that Jesus would cross barriers to reach?
Jesus offered water that satisfies forever, yet we keep returning to wells that leave us thirsty. What broken cisterns are you drinking from instead of coming to Him?
Jesus knew everything the woman had ever done and still offered her living water. How does it free you to be fully known by Him and still fully welcomed?
The Father is seeking worshipers who worship in spirit and truth. What would it look like for your worship to depend less on place and mood and more on the Spirit and the truth of Christ?
The official simply took Jesus at His word. Where is Jesus asking you to trust His word before you can see the outcome?

translationNotes.note: The sixth hour; the BSB footnote explains this means about noon, an unusual time to draw water, hinting at the woman's isolation.
The explanatory clause for Jews do not associate with Samaritans; the BSB footnote notes some manuscripts omit it. It captures the deep estrangement Jesus crosses.
Ripe for harvest renders a word the BSB footnote says is literally white; the fields white for harvest picture the ingathering of people to Christ.
I who speak to you am He renders ego eimi, I am, a phrase that throughout John echoes the divine name and reaches its fullest force in the later I am sayings.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 4:1-54. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
